Title: The haplotype-resolved genome assembly of autotetraploid rhubarb Rheum officinale provides insights into its genome evolution and massive accumulation of anthraquinones

Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Expanded family; some members, especially Scaffold_103.6 and Chr9A.2165, were highly expressed in roots.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Root-preferential expression confirmed by transcriptome and RT-qPCR for selected genes.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Constitutive tissue comparison among roots, stems, and leaves
Common_Genes.Epigenetic_Regulation: Family expansion occurred mainly via transposed duplication; TE-rich genome context may influence expression but no direct TTM3-specific epigenetic mechanism was shown.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: TTM3 was discussed mainly in relation to root development, which may indirectly support accumulation of root-enriched anthraquinones rather than directly catalyzing anthraquinone biosynthesis.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. TTM3 family was strongly expanded in R. officinale compared with related species, mainly through transposed duplication. 2. Several TTM3 copies were highly expressed in roots and validated by RT-qPCR. 3. Authors proposed TTM3, especially Scaffold_103.6, as a key gene for rhizome/root development, indirectly relevant to medicinal tissue yield and metabolite accumulation.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative genomics; duplication analysis; transcriptomics; RT-qPCR
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Likely promotes root/rhizome development, potentially increasing the tissue that accumulates anthraquinones.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful candidate for improving rhizome development and biomass in breeding programs aimed at enhancing medicinal yield.

Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Many ME genes expressed across tissues, with root-biased expression for a subset.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Constitutive tissue comparison among roots, stems, and leaves
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: ME was highlighted as an expanded family linked to metabolism/stress and not directly to a specific nutrient end product in this paper.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ME family was greatly expanded in R. officinale compared with related species. 2. Many copies were expressed, particularly in roots. 3. The paper discusses ME mainly as an expanded metabolism/stress-related family rather than a directly validated anthraquinone gene.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: May support specialized metabolism and root/stress physiology, but direct causal effect on anthraquinone accumulation was not established.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential auxiliary target for improving metabolic robustness, but not yet a direct anthraquinone engineering target.

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Aroma, physiological activity, and roles in stress responses.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: TPS-b and TPS-g genes were highly expressed in leaves.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Leaf-high expression matched monoterpenoid enrichment in leaves.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Constitutive tissue comparison among roots, stems, and leaves
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Although metabolically important, TPS genes were not the central nutrient-metabolism focus compared with anthraquinone genes in this paper.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. TPS genes were identified and classified into five subfamilies. 2. TPS-b and TPS-g showed leaf-biased expression. 3. Their expression pattern was consistent with higher leaf monoterpenoid content.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Associated with elevated monoterpenoid accumulation in leaves.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Can be used to manipulate terpene composition, aroma, and stress-related metabolites.

Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Most OSC genes were highly expressed in roots and stems.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Root/stem-high expression matched triterpene accumulation in roots and stems.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Constitutive tissue comparison among roots, stems, and leaves
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: OSC genes were discussed as key triterpene enzymes but not linked to anthraquinone metabolism.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. OSC genes were identified as key enzymes for triterpene structural diversification. 2. Most OSC genes were highly expressed in roots and stems. 3. This expression matched metabolomic enrichment of triterpenes in roots and stems.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Associated with triterpene accumulation in roots and stems.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Targets for modifying medicinal triterpene composition in rhubarb.

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Medicinal bioactive compounds used in traditional Chinese medicine.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Expanded family; many members expressed, with anthraquinone-pathway genes generally higher in roots than stems and leaves.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Anthraquinone-pathway genes, including EPSP family members, were generally highly expressed in roots.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Constitutive tissue comparison among roots, stems, and leaves
Pathway_Genes.Epigenetic_Regulation: Gene family expansion mainly via transposed duplication for many members; TE insertions broadly affect gene regulation in the genome, but no EPSP-specific epigenetic mechanism was directly shown.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genome-wide candidate identification; comparative gene family analysis; transcriptomics; metabolomics
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Expanded EPSP copy number and root-associated expression likely contribute to enhanced anthraquinone precursor supply and high anthraquinone accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. EPSP was one of nine enzyme families assigned to the shikimate-derived anthraquinone precursor pathway. 2. EPSP family size was strongly expanded in R. officinale versus Fagopyrum species, with 10–15 copies per monoploid genome versus one copy in each comparator. 3. Most expanded EPSP genes arose through transposed duplication, suggesting contribution to massive anthraquinone accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Paper treats EPSP as candidate family-level evidence rather than single-gene biochemical validation.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful target for pathway-stacking to increase anthraquinone precursor flux, especially together with downstream anthraquinone biosynthetic genes.

Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Alleles showed divergent tissue expression; Chr7B.887 was highly expressed in roots whereas corresponding alleles were highly expressed in leaves.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Anthraquinone biosynthetic genes were generally higher in roots than in stems and leaves.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Constitutive tissue comparison among roots, stems, and leaves
Pathway_Genes.Epigenetic_Regulation: Different LTR retrotransposon insertions in genic regions were associated with allele-differential expression; Chr7A.888, Chr7C.867, and Chr7D.884 had an LTR insertion in the last intron, while Chr7B.887 showed root-high expression.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Allele-aware genome assembly; transcriptomics; structural comparison of alleles; metabolomics
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Root-high expression of specific ICS allele(s) likely contributes to elevated anthraquinone biosynthesis in roots.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ICS was assigned to the shikimate branch of anthraquinone biosynthesis. 2. ICS alleles showed strong tissue-expression divergence despite high sequence similarity. 3. LTR insertion differences were associated with altered allele expression patterns, implicating TE-mediated regulation of anthraquinone precursor formation.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Evidence is correlative at allele-expression level; no direct enzymatic assay in this paper.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Allele selection or promoter editing of root-preferential ICS alleles may enhance anthraquinone accumulation.

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Medicinal compounds including emodin- and physcion-related metabolites.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Alleles shared >99.6% identity and identical gene structures, but expression differed: Chr4C.101 highest in leaf, Chr4A.81 lowest, Chr4B.80 and Chr4D.94 intermediate.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Anthraquinone-pathway genes overall were higher in roots than stems and leaves, though MenB alleles showed notable divergence.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Constitutive tissue comparison among roots, stems, and leaves
Pathway_Genes.Epigenetic_Regulation: Allele-differential expression was associated with distinct upstream LTR insertions; two LTRs upstream of Chr4C.101 and one upstream of Chr4A.81.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Allele-aware genome assembly; transcriptomics; upstream structural analysis; metabolomics
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Differential MenB allele expression may modulate precursor supply for anthraquinone accumulation across tissues.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MenB participates in forming 1,4-dihydroxy-2-naphthoyl-CoA in the shikimate-derived anthraquinone pathway. 2. MenB alleles showed marked expression divergence despite highly similar coding sequences. 3. Upstream LTR insertion differences were linked to distinct allele expression patterns, supporting TE-mediated regulation.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: MenB was highlighted as an allele-divergence example rather than a fully functionally validated bottleneck.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Promoter engineering or allele selection of favorable MenB copies could be used to optimize anthraquinone precursor formation.

Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Twenty-two PKS III genes and seven PKC genes showed much lower expression in stems and leaves; many CHS and non-CHS PKS III genes were highly expressed in roots.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Root-biased expression strongly matched high anthraquinone content in roots.
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Type III polyketide synthase/chalcone synthase superfamily
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Constitutive tissue comparison among roots, stems, and leaves
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as key control/committed-step candidates for anthraquinone formation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Phylogenetic analysis; transcriptomics; metabolomics; comparative expression profiling
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Root-preferential expression of specific PKS III genes is associated with high anthraquinone accumulation and identifies likely key biosynthetic genes.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Type III PKS genes were strongly implicated in the polyketide branch of anthraquinone biosynthesis. 2. Root-biased expression of many CHS and non-CHS PKS III genes paralleled high anthraquinone abundance in roots. 3. Specific candidates Chr1A.4008, Chr1B.3886, Chr1B.3888, Chr1C.3889, Chr1D.3860, Chr5B.4624, Chr5D.4705, and Chr5A.4690 were proposed as key genes.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper emphasizes candidate status based on expression/phylogeny rather than direct in vitro enzyme demonstration within this study.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Top candidates for metabolic engineering and heterologous pathway reconstruction of anthraquinone biosynthesis.

Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Seven PKC genes had much lower expression in stems and leaves than in roots.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Root-biased expression corresponded with abundant anthraquinones in roots.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Constitutive tissue comparison among roots, stems, and leaves
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Root-high PKC expression likely supports anthraquinone scaffold cyclization and contributes to root accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PKC was included among anthraquinone biosynthetic candidate genes in the polyketide pathway. 2. PKC expression was much lower in stems and leaves than in roots. 3. Expression pattern supported a role in tissue-specific anthraquinone accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate partner for PKS III stacking in anthraquinone pathway engineering.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes methylation of emodin to produce physcion, as reported from prior studies and linked here to candidate root-expressed genes.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Fourteen of 24 expressed SAM genes were highly expressed in roots.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Root-high expression matched high physcion content in roots.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Constitutive tissue comparison among roots, stems, and leaves
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transcriptomics; metabolomics; prior literature-supported enzyme assignment
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Root-enriched SAM expression likely promotes physcion biosynthesis from emodin, contributing to high physcion accumulation in roots.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SAM genes were linked to methylation of emodin to physcion. 2. Fourteen of the 24 expressed SAM genes were highly expressed in roots. 3. Their expression pattern paralleled elevated physcion levels in roots.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Catalytic assignment cited from previous literature; this paper provides expression-metabolite association.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful target for increasing physcion content through root-specific activation or gene stacking.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes glucosylation of emodin to produce emodin-6-O-glucoside, inferred from evolutionary relationship to previously characterized RpUGT1/UGT73BE14.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Seven evolutionarily related UGT73 genes showed higher expression in roots.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Root-biased expression matched abundant emodin-6-O-glucoside in roots.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Constitutive tissue comparison among roots, stems, and leaves
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Phylogenetic relationship to known RpUGT1; transcriptomics; metabolomics
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Root-high UGT73 expression likely promotes emodin glucosylation and accumulation of emodin-6-O-glucoside in roots.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Seven UGT73 genes related to known emodin glucosyltransferase RpUGT1 were identified in R. officinale. 2. These genes were more highly expressed in roots. 3. Their expression matched the abundant emodin-6-O-glucoside content in roots.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The directly characterized enzyme RpUGT1 was from Rheum palmatum in prior literature; the present paper reports R. officinale homologs, so source and applied species are both R. officinale here for the focal genes analyzed.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong candidates for engineering anthraquinone glycoside composition and improving stable product accumulation.
